common_name: Senita Cactus (Old Man Cactus, Whisker Cactus)

planting.note: Plant container-grown stock in spring in very well-drained, lean, rocky, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; senita cactus is native to the Sonoran Desert of Baja California and the Arizona/Sonora borderlands and requires warm temperatures year-round — it is frost-sensitive below 25°F.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, rocky, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; water rarely — once established, no supplemental irrigation needed in dry climates. Pachycereus schottii (senita cactus or old man cactus) is a distinctive Sonoran Desert columnar cactus forming multi-stemmed clumps of slender, grey-green columns up to 10–15 feet tall. The most distinctive feature is the 'whiskered' appearance of mature stems: the upper portion of older columns becomes densely clothed in long, soft, grey, hair-like bristles (spines), creating a dramatic, shaggy, old-man beard appearance — the source of the common names 'senita' (Spanish: 'old woman') and 'whisker cactus.' In contrast, younger portions of the same stem have few, short spines. Flowers are pink, small, and nocturnal; fruits are red and edible. The senita cactus has a unique, obligate mutualistic relationship with the senita moth (Upiga virescens) — the moth pollinates the flowers while simultaneously laying eggs in them, providing a fascinating example of coevolution. A striking specimen for desert and xeriscape gardens in zone 9–12 where temperatures stay above 25°F.
sources: University of Arizona Extension — Sonoran Desert Plants
Cactus and Succulent Society of America

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container stock in early spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline, moderately fertile soil in full sun to part shade; cornelian cherry is exceptionally cold-hardy and adaptable, tolerating a wide range of soil conditions and even modest drought once established.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline, moderately fertile soil in full sun to part shade; cornelian cherry tolerates a wide range of soil conditions. Cornus mas (cornelian cherry dogwood) is a large, multi-stemmed European and Asian shrub or small tree producing one of the very earliest spring flower displays of any hardy plant — tiny, bright-yellow flowers clustered in small, button-like heads that cover the bare branches in February–March (often while snow still falls) before any other spring-blooming shrub. The flowers are followed by oval, cherry-like fruits that ripen to deep scarlet-red in August, tasting like a tart-sweet cranberry crossed with a sour cherry — used in jams, jellies, liqueurs (the Georgian chacha), and sauces throughout Eastern Europe and the Caucasus, where the sour flavor is prized in cooking much like tamarind. The tree is also fall-ornamental with excellent red-pink fall foliage and attractive peeling bark. 'Golden Glory' and 'Redstone' are widely grown cultivars. At the warm edge of its range (zone 8), it grows best with afternoon shade and consistent moisture; it is marginal in zone 9 and does best in the cooler northern parts of zone 8.
sources: University of Maryland Cooperative Extension — Edible Landscaping
